data.title: Rick Scott unveils legislation requiring country of origin on drug labeling
data.description:
Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.) on Thursday unveiled legislation that would require prescription drug labels to include information about where medications originated from.

Scott’s bill, shown first to The Hill, is titled the “Consumer Labeling for Enhanced API Reporting and Legitimate Accountability for Base Entity Listings (CLEAR LABELS) Act.”

The legislation would require prescription drug labels to disclose the original manufacturers of generic drugs. Its release coincides with a hearing on “truth in labeling” in the Senate Special Committee on Aging, which Scott chairs.

“Families want to feel safe; they want to know the drugs they take are safe, and they want to support American companies and American jobs. It is alarming just how many medicines Americans depend on are made overseas, in places like Communist China and India with limited transparency and oversight, leaving patients blindsided about where their drugs come from and unknowingly putting themselves at increased risk of death,” Scott said in a statement. 

According to the U.S. Pharmacopeia, generic drugs make up 90 percent of U.S. prescriptions, with roughly a third of generics originating from India, about a fifth originating from the European Union and about 16 percent having unknown origins.

The legislation builds off a bipartisan report that the committee released which highlighted the U.S.’s growing dependence on pharmaceutical products manufactured overseas. The report noted that the U.S. is particularly reliant on China and Indian for products like antibiotics and generic drugs, respectively.

Special committee Ranking Member Kirsten Gillibrand (D-N.Y.) helped direct the report, which recommended the development of a federal buyer’s market that prioritized American-made APIs as well as adding countries of origin on drug labels.

data.title: Live updates: Homan says Minneapolis operation will be 'by the book'; Trump holds Cabinet meeting
data.description:
Trump administration border czar Tom Homan said at a press conference Thursday morning that the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis will be done “by the book” as President Trump looks to “fix” it.

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on. What we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” Homan said Thursday.

The Senate is also scrambling as a Jan. 30 partial shutdown deadline quickly approaches and Democrats dig their heels in on Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding.

After Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by immigration officials in Minneapolis, the fight over President Trump’s immigration enforcement operations has reached a fever pitch across the country and on Capitol Hill. Some Senate Republicans, however, have said they are open to splitting off the DHS funding from a six-bill package to avoid another shutdown.

Pretti’s killing, as well as the fatal shooting of Renee Good in the same city earlier this month, has called Trump’s position on immigration — which has historically been the president’s strongest issue — into question, as the GOP looks to maintain its majority in this year’s midterm elections.

Trump on Thursday morning will host a Cabinet meeting at the White House. He will later join first lady Melania Trump at the premiere of her documentary at the Kennedy Center.

Follow live below.

data.title: How to avoid a new nuclear arms race — at least for now
data.description:
On Feb. 5, New START, the last treaty limiting U.S. and Russian strategic nuclear arms, will expire — a dark milestone. 

 As war rages in Ukraine, tensions mount over Taiwan, China builds up its nuclear forces and Russia builds new weapons — from an intercontinental nuclear torpedo to a nuclear-powered cruise missile — there will be no accords holding back a dangerous three-way nuclear arms buildup.

But President Trump has an opportunity to put off such a nuclear competition and explore whether new accords can help avoid it. Russian President Vladimir Putin has offered to stay within the limits of New START for at least a year if the U.S. does likewise, and to start talks on the full range of factors affecting nuclear dangers. 

It is not a great offer. For now, Putin hasn’t included any on-site inspections to verify what each side is doing — although U.S. intelligence has a good count of Russia’s nuclear launchers, warheads are trickier, Also, a year does not buy much real predictability.

Moreover, for now, China has no interest in discussing limits on its nuclear buildup, which is still far behind U.S. and Russian force levels. 

But such a pause would offer the critical variable of time to discuss whether there is enough common interest and willingness to compromise to avoid a renewed nuclear competition. There might be. Spending hundreds of billions on new nuclear weapons would not make Russia, China or the U.S. more secure. 

Putin is building a war economy, which can churn out a lot of weapons, but he knows his economy is one-tenth the size of the U.S. economy. He wants to focus on conventional weapons, and that makes an unrestrained nuclear competition a bad idea for him.

China has an economy closer to America’s size, and an unrivaled manufacturing capacity. But it, too, would be worse off if its buildup were to provoke an American buildup in response and a collapse of nuclear restraints.

Although some American nuclear advocates are pushing for bringing hundreds of warheads out of storage and putting them on missiles to be prepared for nuclear conflict with both Russia and China, Trump has a different view.  

“There’s no reason for us to be building brand new nuclear weapons,” he has argued, since “we already have so many. You could destroy the world 50 times over, 100 times over.” Rather than China, Russia and the U.S. all building up their forces, he has called for reaching new “denuclearization” deals, reducing the risk of nuclear war and “spending on other things that are actually, hopefully, much more productive.”  

Trump has played down New START’s expiration, hoping for a “better agreement” to replace it, but has said Putin’s offer “sounds like a good idea.” Putin’s spokesman has said Russia is still “awaiting” a U.S. response.

Despite their common interests, finding paths to nuclear restraint among three major nuclear powers, rather than two, will be a difficult challenge — particularly as non-nuclear technologies from precision conventional weapons to artificial intelligence complicate nuclear balances.  

Both Russia and China are concerned about U.S. missile defenses undermining their nuclear deterrents, and the U.S. is concerned about their pursuit of hypersonic nuclear missiles and weapons to threaten American satellites. Putin’s Russia has violated some past arms control pacts, and China has no experience with negotiating limits on its nuclear forces or accepting inspections.

Those challenges are precisely why the time offered by a temporary deal to stay within the limits of New START could be so important. It’s better to get talks going to explore the options before a buildup builds momentum and effectively gets locked in.

Trump should agree with Putin on a “strategic pause” — and possibly extend it to two or three years, rather than just one, to give talks a chance. Meanwhile, he should also push Putin to include inspections or voluntary visits, after legal arrangements for inspections included in the treaty expire.

Even if talks prove fruitless, and the U.S. decides it needs to build up, that will take time. So pausing for two or three years to see what the alternatives might be would pose little obstacle.

Trump can take a major step for both American and global security by announcing a deal to keep both U.S. and Russian nuclear forces on a leash for now and launching talks with both Russia and China to explore whether his “better agreement” can really be reached.

Matthew Bunn is the James R. Schlesinger Professor of the Practice of Energy, National Security, and Foreign Policy at Harvard University’s Kennedy School of Government.

data.title: Milan mayor: 'ICE's image is terrible'
data.description:
Milan Mayor Giuseppe Sala on Wednesday condemned the presence of  Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) at the Olympic Winter Games after weeks of violent incidents with U.S. citizens in Minnesota.

“ICE’s image is terrible,” Sala told The Washington Post.

His words echo those of other officials in Italy who have rejected ICE’s involvement at the international event, including lawmakers who have requested guarantees that immigration enforcement will not be conducted at the sporting events.

“The Democratic Party has submitted a parliamentary question to [Italy’s interior minister Matteo] Piantedosi, requesting immediate clarification and the exclusion of any operational presence of ICE on Italian soil,” Matteo Mauri, a member of Parliament and the party’s lead on security, said in a statement to the Post.

The U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS), which houses ICE, has sought to quell concerns by confirming that ICE will not be conducting immigration activities at the Games.

“Obviously, ICE does not conduct immigration enforcement operations in foreign countries,” the DHS wrote in a Tuesday post on the social platform X. 

“At the Olympics, ICE’s Homeland Security Investigations is supporting the U.S. Department of State’s Diplomatic Security Service and host nation to vet and mitigate risks from transnational criminal organizations,” it added.

data.title: Warner: 'Why is Tulsi Gabbard at an FBI raid on an election office in Fulton County?'
data.description:
Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) on Wednesday shared Reuters’s photo of Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard during the FBI’s search of 2020 voting records at the Fulton County, Ga., elections office, questioning why she was there.

“Why is Tulsi Gabbard at an FBI raid on an election office in Fulton County?” Warner asked in a post on social platform X, which included a video.

Reuters photographer Elijah Nouvelage captured the photo of Gabbard wearing a baseball cap and talking into a smartphone while next to a vehicle loaded with boxes, her eyes turned toward the camera.

Warner said there were two explanations for why Gabbard would be at the election offices.

“Either Director Gabbard believes there was a legitimate foreign intelligence nexus — in which case she is in clear violation of her obligation under the law to keep the intelligence committees ‘fully and currently informed’ of relevant national security concerns — or she is once again demonstrating her utter lack of fitness for the office that she holds by injecting the nonpartisan intelligence community she is supposed to be leading into a domestic political stunt designed to legitimize conspiracy theories that undermine our democracy,” he wrote in a response to his first post.

“Either is a serious breach of trust that further underscores why she is totally unqualified to hold a position that demands sound judgment, apolitical independence, and a singular focus on keeping Americans safe,” he added.

FBI Deputy Director Andrew Bailey was also at the site of the search, The Hill’s sister network NewsNation reported.

An official told NewsNation that Gabbard “has a pivotal role in election security and protecting the integrity of our elections against interference, including operations targeting voting systems, databases, and election infrastructure. She has and will continue to take action on President Trump’s directive to secure our elections and work with our interagency partners to do so.”

The FBI said agents executed “a court authorized law enforcement action” at the county’s main office in Union City. FBI spokesperson Jenna Sellitto said boxes loaded on the trucks contained ballots.

Agents sought 2020 election records, Fulton County spokesperson Jessica Corbitt-Dominguez said.

“We don’t know why they took them, and we don’t know where they’re taking them to,” county Board of Commissioners Chairman Robb Pitts said.

Fulton County played a pivotal role following President Trump’s loss in the 2020 election. Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis (D) indicted Trump on racketeering charges in 2023 for allegedly conspiring to subvert the election results and win Georgia to stay in office. The case was dropped in 2025 ahead of the president’s return to the White House and after Willis was disqualified from the case.

The county became the target of a complaint issued by the Justice Department (DOJ) last month over information related to the 2020 election. The DOJ requested “all used and void ballots, stubs of all ballots, signature envelopes, and corresponding envelope digital files from the 2020 General Election in Fulton County” to investigate “compliance with federal law.”

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Frey doubles down on Minneapolis sanctuary city policy
data.description:
Mayor Jacob Frey (D) defended Minneapolis’ sanctuary city policy on Wednesday evening, saying the decision was made out of a concern for safety. 

“We want people who are undocumented to have the confidence to call 911 without the fear that they’ll get deported in doing so,” Frey said during a CNN town hall. “That is a safety strategy. That is not an immigration strategy. That is a safety strategy.

Sanctuary cities are places that limit or completely resist the level to which their local law enforcement cooperates with federal officials on immigration enforcement. Over 200 U.S. cities or counties in 13 states have sanctuary policies in place, according to the Center for Immigration Studies.

The Trump administration has criticized these policies, with the Department of Justice releasing a list of sanctuary jurisdictions last August that Attorney General Pam Bondi said “put American citizens at risk by design.”

Several weeks ago, President Trump announced that he would strip federal funding from cities and states with these policies, beginning on Feb. 1.

“ALL THEY DO IS BREED CRIME AND VIOLENCE!” the president wrote on Truth Social. “If States want them, they will have to pay for them! MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!!!”

The CNN town hall revolved around the issue of the Trump administration’s massive immigration operation in the state of Minnesota. During operations in Minneapolis, federal agents shot and killed two Minnesota residents and shot a migrant Venezuelan man in the leg.

An ICE agent fatally shot Renee Good, a mother of three, on Jan. 7. VA hospital intensive care nurse Alex Pretti was shot by federal agents on Saturday. The Department of Homeland Security said in both instances that its officers were acting in self-defense — a claim local and state officials have disputed, calling for a full investigation. 

Mass protests erupted across the city in response to these killings and Democratic state leaders have led forceful calls for DHS to remove federal agents from the state. 

A Minnesota resident, who said Pretti cared for his father at a Veterans Affairs hospital, asked Frey during Wednesday’s town hall when DHS would pull their agents out of Minnesota.

“Look, we don’t have a timeline as to when and how it will be drawn down,” Frey said. “And I know that’s as frustrating and as concerning to me as it is so many other people in our country.”

In a Wednesday morning interview with NBC News, Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz said there is “no evidence” that federal agents have begun pulling out of the state. This followed a Monday phone conversation with President Trump, in which Walz said the president promised to talk to DHS about scaling back the numbers of agents in Minnesota. 

Frey said the operation’s timeline would “be a better question for the federal government” and criticized the immigration crackdowns, saying they’re “not about safety.”

“We’re going to do everything possible that we can to advocate, to push and to stand up for the neighbors who we love so dearly,” Frey said.

data.title: Kennedy Center's new programming head resigns days after hiring announcement
data.description:
The new head of artistic programming at the Kennedy Center, Kevin Couch, has resigned after just weeks on the job, according to several reports. 

The Kennedy Center announced on Jan. 16 that Couch, the former programming head for a UK-based theater owner and production company, had been named its senior vice president of artistic programming. His hiring was promoted again in a Jan. 22 post on the social platform X.

At the time, Couch said he was “honored” to join the center, and interim President Richard Grenell said Couch would bring a “clear-eyed approach to curating a roster of compelling shows that invite and inspire all audiences.”

But Couch confirmed to the Washington Post on Wednesday that he stepped down from the position, declining to provide any further information.

The Kennedy Center did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment.

His exit marks the latest in a series of departures from the arts and cultural institution since President Trump took it over early last year, replacing several board members and naming himself chair, then rebranding the center with his name on it.

Renowned opera singer Renée Fleming left her position as artistic adviser at large; musician Ben Folds resigned as artistic adviser to the National Symphony Orchestra; and television writer and producer Shonda Rhimes also stepped down as the board’s treasurer shortly after Trump announced his takeover.

That list also included Couch’s predecessor, Jeffrey Finn, who resigned last fall after nearly a decade of overseeing the center’s theater programming, according to the New York Times.

News of Couch’s resignation came just one day after composer Philip Glass withdrew his newest work, Symphony No. 15 “Lincoln,” from the Kennedy Center over conflicts with leadership, joining a growing number of artists and performers who have canceled appearances or refused to play at the venue.

data.title: Minnesota judge drops demand for ICE chief to appear while criticizing agency
data.description:
Minnesota’s chief federal judge backed off his threat to potentially hold the head of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in contempt for violating court orders but scorched the agency’s conduct amid the Trump administration’s crackdown in the state.

Chief U.S. District Judge Patrick Schiltz cancelled a hearing set for Friday where acting ICE director Todd Lyons would have been forced to take the stand to explain the agency’s aggressive surge.

“That does not end the Court’s concerns, however,” the judge wrote in an order Wednesday.

Schiltz pointed to a list of 96 orders he said ICE had violated across more than 70 cases since the crackdown, dubbed Operation Metro Surge, began in Minnesota. He said the list should “give pause to anyone — no matter his or her political beliefs — who cares about the rule of law.”

“ICE has likely violated more court orders in January 2026 than some federal agencies have violated in their entire existence,” the judge said.

The Hill has requested comment from ICE.

The chief judge’s breaking point came in the case of Juan Hugo Tobay Robles, who had informed the court that he remained detained despite a judge’s ruling that the Trump administration must afford him a bond hearing or release him.

Schiltz said the court had been “extremely patient” with the administration, even as thousands of ICE officers descended on Minnesota to detain noncitizens without a plan for the hundreds of legal challenges that were “sure to result.”

Despite assurances from the government that court orders would be honored, “the violations continue,” he said on Monday, scheduling the hearing and calling Lyons to testify under threat of contempt.

But after the hearing was set, Robles’ attorneys and the government filed a joint statement indicating that he had been released, prompting Schiltz to cancel the proceeding.

“The Court warns ICE that future noncompliance with court orders may result in future show cause orders requiring the personal appearances of Lyons or other government officials,” the judge wrote.

“ICE is not a law unto itself,” he added.

ICE and Border Patrol have detained numerous people, including migrants and protesters, since their operation in Minnesota began last month. The Justice Department charged 16 protesters on Wednesday with assaulting officers or interfering with federal immigration officers.

Local and state officials have pushed back against the federal presence, particularly in the wake of the fatal shootings of U.S. citizens Renee Good and Alex Pretti. State and city officials have sued to get ICE out of the Twin Cities.

data.title: Will Republicans break their losing streak on health care reform?
data.description:
Despite a handful of promising drives, Republicans have a losing record on health care reform over the past two decades. Since the passage of ObamaCare, the GOP has repeatedly failed to successfully field an alternative vision — leaving more Americans with insurance cards but fewer who can afford it. Will Republicans prevail this time around with the so-called “Great Healthcare Plan” released in January?

Republican margins in Congress are extremely slim and getting members to coalesce behind one blueprint will be like herding cats. But there is hope given conservatives were able to eke out a tax cut win last summer under similar conditions. To borrow a line from “Dumb and Dumber”, “so you’re telling me there’s a chance.”

Most of the ideas being floated are strong. Fostering more price transparency and addressing cost inefficiencies created by middlemen will certainly ease some financial pain, particularly for prescription drugs. After all, pharmacy benefit managers — which are the intermediaries between drug manufacturers and patients — collect a big chunk of every dollar spent on medicine without offering much value.

But of biggest consequence would be a shake up of how ObamaCare subsidies are distributed. Currently, government aid dollars go directly to insurance companies, with the hope of cheaper premiums for eligible beneficiaries. The roundabout money trail keeps patients in the dark about the true cost of care and makes it more difficult to shop around for the best deal. 

To remedy the backwards dynamic, the Republican proposal would deposit those government subsidies directly into tax advantaged health savings accounts so individuals have more spending control. It’s an adjustment that even Democrats should support. The reform keeps the core concept of ObamaCare in place but restructures it to ensure existing financial aid is being stretched to the max.

Not all Republican proposals are winners, however. As part of the package, the White House is seeking to codify a dangerous government price control precedent into law that will have a chilling effect on medical innovation and patient choice. The so-called “Most Favored Nation” standard aims to peg the price of drugs to rates that are paid in countries overseas — valuations that are often greatly distorted by socialized medicine.

Yes, under such a framework, people in the U.S. could potentially access some brand name medicines at lower prices in the short run. But given that Americans already pay less for generic drugs — which account for nine in 10 prescriptions — the limited upside would be greatly outweighed in the long run by the inevitable consequences of socialism. 

As we’ve seen in Europe, top-down-economics has gutted the free market and hollowed out its medical manufacturing capacity, leading to drug shortages and delayed access to innovative treatments. Now, the continent relies on medical breakthroughs developed by American companies trickling down to its residents. The U.S. would be wise to avoid such self-sabotage. 

Republicans have an opportunity to get solid health care reform over the finish line this year before the midterm elections rebalance power in Washington. By focusing on policies that promote transparency, increase patient choice, and preserve free market incentives, the GOP can maximize their odds of success. Other misguided ideas should be left on Capitol Hill’s cutting room floor.

Dr. Tom Price served as the 23rd U.S. secretary of Health and Human Services and is a former member of Congress from Georgia.

data.title: Watch live: Trump holds Cabinet meeting amid ICE scrutiny, DHS funding battle
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday morning will hold a Cabinet meeting at the White House as the administration faces scrutiny over its immigration enforcement tactics.

Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem has taken on the brunt of the criticism after she defended immigration officers involved in the fatal shootings of two Minneapolis residents earlier this month. Many Democrats, and some Republicans, have called for Noem’s ouster or resignation.

Border czar Tom Homan on Thursday vowed “to fix” operations in the state after he was sent to Minnesota to oversee immigration enforcement.

Funding for DHS is also on the line, as a partial government shutdown looms, though Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and Trump are reportedly eyeing a deal.

The meeting also comes after Secretary of State Marco Rubio briefed lawmakers about the administration’s moves in Venezuela, weeks after the U.S. captured its leader Nicolás Maduro. Trump’s foreign policy moves, including talks of taking over Greenland, have also been under the microscope.

The event is scheduled to begin at 11 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.
